title: Facet teardown does not retract assertions/Observe handles
description: Facet lifetimes are not wired to assertion lifetimes: `terminate_facet_impl/2` only deletes entities and children (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:652-687) and the actor `terminate/2` callback just cleans dataflow fields (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:876-884), leaving any outbound assertions or Observe subscriptions in the dataspace. Turn-initiated asserts are not tracked either—`process_action/2` explicitly avoids recording them (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:781-786) and `assert_value/3` generates a handle that is never returned or stored (lib/absynthe.ex:411-415)—so there is no way to retract them when a facet dies. In syndicate-rs each facet owns its assertions and retracts them automatically on drop, ensuring fate-sharing; here stale assertions/subscriptions will persist after facet/actor termination.

title: Assertions don't retract on asserting actor crash
description: The dissertation promises fate-sharing for assertions: when an actor dies, its whole assertion set is withdrawn (see §4.1, lines around "When an actor crashes, all its assertions are automatically retracted"). Our dataspace never learns when an asserting actor exits. Handles only carry an actor_id, and the dataspace/Bags track handles with no owner monitoring (, ).  sends an Assert event but does not register the caller or monitor it, and dataspace state is only cleaned when a peer explicitly calls  or the asserting actor happens to run its own  and send remote retractions. If a peer process crashes or is killed, its assertions remain in the Bag indefinitely and observers never see the required retractions, breaking the fault-signaling property of the model. We need to track assertion ownership by PID and auto-retract on DOWN (including local and remote assertions), so crashes cannot leave stale knowledge in the dataspace.

title: Sync semantics don’t acknowledge correctly
description: Dataspace  replies with  regardless of target (), sending the ack back to the initiator instead of the dataspace’s own ref and without waiting for prior events on the target. In syndicate-rs, sync is a barrier on the target facet/actor that replies to the requester after all prior events are processed. Our current behavior can loop a sync back to the requester without involving the target and doesn’t guarantee ordering; we need to align the barrier/ack semantics with the dissertation/spec.

comments.author: femtomc
comments.text:
Fixed documentation drift issues raised in review:
- Updated lib/absynthe/core/entity.ex:206-213 module doc to describe the new on_sync default behavior
- Updated README.md examples to note that on_sync uses default behavior (sends synced message)
- Updated doc examples in lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex and lib/absynthe.ex

All 326 tests + 36 doctests pass.

title: Implement observer match tracking and capture delivery
description: Absynthe.Dataspace.Observer docs describe active match tracking and captures, but dataspace never records matches or propagates capture bindings. Wire dataspace notifications to populate Observer.active_handles, include captures in notifications (not just assertions), and ensure retractions update match state, matching syndicate-rs behavior.

comments.author: femtomc
comments.text:
REVIEW: Active handle cleanup fails with refcounted assertions

- `handle_regular_assertion` only indexes the first handle of a value (`lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:374-380`), so the skeleton tracks a canonical handle.
- If that canonical handle is retracted while other handles remain, the skeleton still holds it. When the last remaining handle is retracted, `Skeleton.remove_assertion/2` looks up the last handle (`lib/absynthe/dataspace/skeleton.ex:231-259`), hits the empty lookup branch, and returns no observer info/cleanup. We skip `active_handles` removal and notifications (`lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:416-430`), and the skeleton keeps the stale canonical handle. Observers never see the final retract and `active_handles` leak.
- This refcounted path (retracting handles out of order) isn’t exercised in tests; current coverage only covers single-handle flows.
- Docs for `Skeleton.add_assertion/3` and `remove_assertion/2` still describe the old return shapes without observer_ids (`lib/absynthe/dataspace/skeleton.ex:162-166`, `219-221`), which no longer match the implementation.

comments.author: femtomc
comments.text: REVIEW: Fixed - addressed all review findings. Updated stale doc examples in Bag module to use new {:removed, bag, assertion} return type. All 317 tests + 36 doctests pass.

title: Actor docs claim all events are async
description: The Actor module doc says “All events are delivered asynchronously via handle_cast/2” (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:200-206), but assert/retract calls execute turns synchronously inside GenServer.call/3 (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:513-546) and local delivery uses direct dispatch in the caller process. The docs should clarify which paths are synchronous vs async (calls vs casts), or the implementation should be updated to match the documented behavior. Syndicate-rs documents turn delivery semantics precisely; our docs currently mislead users about when they’re blocking.

title: Observe patterns ignore named captures
description: Absynthe.capture/1 builds `{:symbol, "$name"}` when given a name (lib/absynthe.ex:517-523), but the dataspace pattern compiler only treats the bare `{:symbol, "$"}` token as a capture (lib/absynthe/dataspace/pattern.ex:81-83). As a result the README-style `capture(:name)` patterns compile to literal symbol constraints and never match any assertions, so Observe subscriptions can’t bind or even match when a named capture is present. In syndicate-rs, `$name` captures bind correctly and drive notifications; parity requires recognizing the named form here.

title: Assertion handles aren't globally unique
description: Handles are just actor-local integers (lib/absynthe/assertions/handle.ex:14-38) and each actor counter starts at 0 (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:513-520). Dataspaces index by Handle alone (e.g., lib/absynthe/assertions/bag.ex:84-146; lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:104-142), so two actors asserting to the same dataspace with handle 0 collide and retractions can drop the wrong fact. In syndicate-rs handles are namespaced by publisher, preventing cross-actor collisions; parity requires a globally unique or actor-qualified handle.

title: Observer docs promise match tracking that isn’t implemented
description: Module docs for Absynthe.Dataspace.Observer describe active match tracking (active_handles, add_match/remove_match/match_count) and capture delivery (lib/absynthe/dataspace/observer.ex:1-170), but the dataspace never calls those functions—observe assertions are stored in Skeleton only and Observer structs’ active_handles stay empty (lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:320-360). Users reading the docs will expect match state and capture delivery that doesn’t exist. Either adjust the docs to describe the actual behavior or wire the match tracking up; syndicate-rs delivers captures and maintains subscription state.

title: Capability attenuation is not enforced
description: Synit SAM docs (“Object capabilities for access control” in syndicated-actor-model.html) define capabilities as (actor, entity, attenuation), with attenuation pattern-checking assertions/messages; syndicate-rs enforces this via Cap.rewrite/attenuate and Caveat checking (e.g. syndicate/src/actor.rs attenuate/rewrite, rewrite.rs). Our implementation exposes full-power refs: lib/absynthe/core/ref.ex has an  field marked “reserved for future use”, and neither Actor.deliver/commit nor Dataspace/Dataspace.handle_* performs any attenuation or filtering. Any ref grants full authority to assert/observe/message anything, violating the model’s security story. We need to implement attenuation parsing, checked rewrites, and enforcement on outbound/inbound assertions/messages (including Observe subscriptions).
design:
Implementation plan for capability attenuation enforcement:
1) Data model: define an attenuation spec that mirrors syndicate-rs Caveat/Pattern/Template (rewrite.rs). Represent as Elixir structs; encode/decode via Preserves so attenuated refs can be sent/embedded. Extend Absynthe.Core.Ref to store a validated attenuation chain (checked at construction/parsing).
2) Checker: implement attenuation validation (pattern binding rules, no binding under negation, template refs in-range) and a rewrite executor that takes an assertion/message value, applies the attenuation chain in reverse, and yields Some(filtered/transformed) or nil to reject.
3) Enforcement: in Actor.deliver/commit, before delivering Assert/Message events, run attenuation on the ref: outbound (when we send) and inbound (when we receive) must both rewrite or drop. Dataspace Observe assertions must also be filtered by the ref’s attenuation.
4) API surface: add helpers to attenuate a ref (new attenuated Ref) and to parse refs with attenuation from Preserves embedded values. For now, keep in-memory only (sturdy refs tracked separately).
5) Tests: unit tests for attenuation validation/rewrite; integration tests showing a ref attenuated to a pattern rejects non-matching asserts/messages and allows matching ones; Observe attenuation test for subscriptions.

title: Message delivery ignores dataspace interest semantics
description: In the dataspace model (§4.4, def. 4.13), message actions are broadcast and only delivered to actors that have previously asserted interest; interest also scopes conversations. Our implementation routes messages directly to a specific Ref via Actor.send_message/3 and Protocol.Event.message/2, bypassing the dataspace and any subscription/interest tracking. This prevents the interest-gated, many-party conversations described in the thesis and makes messages behave like plain point-to-point actor mail instead of transient knowledge in the dataspace. We need message routing through the dataspace with interest-based delivery and projection, or at least a subscription mechanism that mirrors the model’s message semantics.

title: Missing tests for capability system (Caveat, SturdyRef, Rewrite)
Make assert/retract delivery async to match Actor docs
description: Actor docs state all events are delivered asynchronously via handle_cast/2, but assert/retract currently run turns inside GenServer.call/3 (lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:513-546) and synchronously dispatch to local entities. Align behavior with the docs by making client assertions/retractions enqueue async delivery (or equivalent non-blocking path) while preserving current semantics for remote delivery and state tracking.

title: No sturdy references or macaroon-style capability attenuation
description: Synit/SAM wire docs (protocol.html#capabilities-on-the-wire, #attenuation) and syndicate-rs implement durable capabilities as signed SturdyRefs with caveat chains (see syndicate/src/sturdy.rs, rewrite.rs, actor.rs Cap::attenuate), enabling mint/attenuate/validate and membrane rewriting of embedded refs when crossing trust boundaries. Absynthe has only in-memory Refs with a reserved attenuation field and no encoding, signing, or attenuation validation. We can’t hand out durable, attenuated capabilities or enforce caveats at receipt, which breaks the security story and interop with Syndicate protocol/gatekeeper patterns.
design:
Implementation plan for sturdy references / durable attenuated capabilities:
1) SturdyRef format: mirror syndicate-rs (syndicate/src/sturdy.rs): parameters = {oid, caveats, sig}. Choose HMAC(BLAKE2s) over packed Preserves of oid + caveat chain; store caveats as the attenuation list (reusing attenuation checker from absynthe-6b4).
2) API: mint(root_ref, key) -> sturdyref; attenuate(sturdyref, caveats) -> sturdyref; validate_and_materialize(sturdyref, key) -> Ref with checked attenuation. Provide hex/text encode/decode helpers.
3) Gatekeeper/membrane: add a small module to rewrite embedded refs on send/receive: when sending outbound, embed sturdyrefs with applied attenuation; when receiving, validate signature, apply caveats, and rewrite embedded refs to live Refs (or reject if invalid). Keep key management pluggable (caller supplies key ring / callback).
4) Wire/runtime integration: extend Preserves embedded codec to encode/decode sturdyrefs; allow Actor/Dataspace to accept embedded sturdyrefs and materialize them before delivery, using attenuation enforcement (absynthe-6b4). Reject invalid signatures/attenuations.
5) Tests: roundtrip mint/attenuate/validate; rejection of bad signatures and invalid caveats; membrane rewrite that strips authority when attenuation doesn’t match; integration test that a sturdyref limited to a pattern only permits those assertions/messages.

title: No turn action for spawning facets/entities
description: Turn actions are limited to Event assert/retract/message/sync and field updates (lib/absynthe/protocol/event.ex; lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex:805-858) and the TODO still notes missing spawn handling. Absynthe.Core.Turn exposes no action to create entities or facets, so entity callbacks cannot spawn new conversations transactionally. In syndicate-rs spawning facets/entities is a first-class turn effect; parity requires a spawn action with commit-time processing and facet tracking.

title: README overpromises Preserves support
description: README claims “full support for the Preserves data format” (README.md:11), but dataspace pattern handling lacks set support (lib/absynthe/dataspace/pattern.ex:222-254 omits {:set,...} in extract_path) and drops capture environments in notifications (lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:410-424). Until those gaps are closed, the README should spell out the limitations instead of promising full coverage, to align expectations with current behavior and syndicate-rs parity.

title: Observer notifications drop capture bindings
description: Skeleton matching returns capture lists, but the dataspace ignores them when sending notifications: both `notify_observer_of_existing/3` and `notify_observers_of_publish/4` throw away `_captures` and emit bare `Event.assert` calls (lib/absynthe/dataspace/dataspace.ex:410-424). That means even if patterns contain `$` captures, observers never learn the bound values. In syndicate-rs the observed assertion carries its match environment, so consumers can read captures; parity requires threading the bindings through to observers.

title: Assertion model lacks state-change notifications
description: The thesis defines assertion publication as state-change notifications (SCNs) that replace the asserting actor’s entire assertion set and project a snapshot to interested peers (see §4.1 and rules newtable/bc). Our implementation treats assertions as independent add/remove operations on handles in a bag (modules: lib/absynthe/core/actor.ex assert/3, lib/absynthe/assertions/bag.ex), with no per-actor set replacement or projection. An actor that wants to replace its state must manually retract old handles, so stale assertions can leak and observers never get a consistent SCN reflecting the whole set. We need per-actor assertion sets and SCN-style replacement semantics (and projected notifications) to match the dataspace model.

title: SturdyRef attenuation appends caveats instead of prepending (breaks outer-first semantics)
Add credit-based internal flow control
description: In `syndicate-rs`, actors track per-activity debt using `Account`/`LoanedItem` (see `syndicate/src/actor.rs` + `doc/flow-control.md`), and input tasks pause when outstanding debt exceeds thresholds, so fan-out is charged back to the origin and the server throttles fast senders. Absynthe actors/dataspaces currently drain the GenServer mailbox with no debt accounting or backpressure; a hot producer can overrun consumers and memory. We should add a credit-based flow-control layer (per-actor accounts, loaned items tied to turn processing) and integrate it with dataspace routing so bursts get throttled. Include metrics/debt reporter hooks and tests that demonstrate throttling under load.

title: Implement Syndicate network protocol/broker
description: `syndicate-rs` ships a full distributed broker (`syndicate-server/src/main.rs`) with TCP/Unix relay listeners, gatekeeper, resolution client, HTTP router, and config watcher services. The core protocol/relay implementation lives in `syndicate/src/relay.rs` and moves caps/handles across the wire. Absynthe is intentionally single-node today (README, `Absynthe.Core.Actor` docs) with no transport or remote dataspace support. We need a server/broker that speaks the Syndicate protocol, can accept TCP/Unix connections, and tunnels dataspace traffic (assert/retract/message/sync) across nodes with the membrane/handle mapping that the Rust relay uses. Include gatekeeper/sturdy-ref resolution so remote clients can be authorized, and cover the basics with a cross-node ping-pong or presence example + tests.

comments.author: femtomc
comments.text: Worth supporting both TCP and Unix sockets. TCP is needed for cross-host peers; Unix sockets give local-only clients lower latency/overhead plus filesystem ACLs and avoid opening ports. `syndicate-rs` already ships both listener types: `syndicate-server/src/services/tcp_relay_listener.rs` (wired via `--port` in `main.rs`) and `syndicate-server/src/services/unix_relay_listener.rs` (wired via `--socket`). When designing our broker/relay, plan for both transports so local CLIs/sidecars can stay on Unix and remote nodes use TCP.

comments.author: femtomc
comments.text: Design doc added at docs/broker_relay_design.md covering broker/relay architecture, transports, gatekeeper/sturdy refs, flow control, and milestones. Created child milestones: absynthe-giw.1 (relay core), absynthe-giw.2 (TCP/Unix listeners + config), absynthe-giw.3 (gatekeeper/sturdy-ref auth), absynthe-giw.4 (observability + cross-node examples).

title: Milestone 1: Relay core (membranes + turn forwarding)
description:
Implement the relay worker and happy-path broker wiring per docs/broker_relay_design.md. Scope:
- Per-connection relay GenServer that speaks the Syndicate protocol (Preserves framing), maintains membranes (remote oid <-> local Ref), refcounts, and attenuation on re-import.
- Forward turn events assert/retract/message/sync into a local broker actor/dataspace; ensure handle lifetime and retractions on disconnect.
- Export/import caps via membranes with garbage collection when refcount hits zero.
- Unix socket happy path first (no TCP yet) with a ping/pong integration test proving cross-process turn delivery.
- Error handling: close relay on framing/protocol errors and retract outstanding assertions.

title: Milestone 2: TCP/Unix listeners and config
description:
Add transport listeners and configuration plumbing per docs/broker_relay_design.md. Scope:
- TCP listener(s) (host/port) and Unix listener(s) (path); supervised accept loops that spawn relay workers.
- CLI/config flags to declare listeners, timeouts/backlog, and gatekeeper binding; service assertions in the broker dataspace similar to syndicate-server RunService.
- Idle-timeout and graceful shutdown to retract outstanding assertions on socket close.
- Logging identifiers for connections and listener lifecycle events.

title: Milestone 3: Gatekeeper and sturdy-ref auth
description:
Wire capability authentication for the broker per docs/broker_relay_design.md. Scope:
- Gatekeeper entity that validates incoming sturdy refs using Absynthe.Core.SturdyRef and resolves them to live Refs for relay imports.
- Attenuation enforcement on import/re-import; reject amplification attempts.
- Optional Noise handshake staging point to mirror syndicate-rs resolution/noise services (document toggle if deferred).
- Example showing a remote client presenting a sturdy ref to reach a service via the relay.

title: Milestone 4: Observability and cross-node examples
description:
Add observability and examples for the broker/relay per docs/broker_relay_design.md. Scope:
- Telemetry/logging for relay lifecycle, bytes in/out, assertion counts, and debt (hook into flow-control metrics once available).
- Debt reporter service and log dataspace assertions similar to syndicate-server.
- Runnable examples: cross-node ping/pong and presence over the relay; include instructions in README/docs.
- Integration tests for handle/cap round-tripping and attenuation enforcement across the relay.

title: Set patterns never match
description: Dataspace pattern compilation emits constraints for set elements (lib/absynthe/dataspace/pattern.ex:333-340), but extract_path/2 has no clause for {:set, ...} values (lib/absynthe/dataspace/pattern.ex:222-254). Any Observe pattern containing a set literal or capture fails constraint checking and never matches assertions. Syndicate-rs supports unordered set matching; parity requires adding set path extraction and matching.

title: Pattern matching allows extra fields/keys
description: Dataspace pattern matcher only checks constraint paths for equality and doesn’t enforce arity/size. Records and sequences with extra fields still match as long as constrained positions match, and dictionaries ignore extra keys (lib/absynthe/dataspace/pattern.ex:156-185, 222-254). In syndicate-rs/Preserves matching, record/sequence arity must match and dictionary patterns are not supersets. This can cause false-positive Observe matches and spurious notifications; matcher should enforce structural size equality.

title: No advertisement protocol for presence discovery
description: Section 8.5.6 introduces the built-in advertisement protocol to signal potential future assertions/publishers and decouple conversation setup from data flow. Our runtime has no advertisement records or support; observers can only see actual assertions or Observe subscriptions. Without advertise/claim, peers cannot discover that a capability or service might come online soon, which is part of the idiomatic Syndicate patterns. Add an advertisement protocol entity/helpers so actors can assert advertise(claim) records and peers can react to their presence/absence.
